text: Three 8mm robotic ports were placed in the 8th intercostal space, as can be seen.
A fourth 12mm port was placed anteriorly in the 6th intercostal space.
An air seal port was placed anteriorly in the 8th intercostal space as well.
Here you can see a moderate amount of hemothorax, which was suctioned, and some pertinent anatomy.
There were three lung lacerations that were oozing.
These were controlled with a figure of eight throws of Vicryl sutures.
Next, we directed our attention to the area of the tracheal injury.
The pleura was divided in order to reveal the trachea.
Asagus vein was divided in order to facilitate the dissection.
the vagus nerve was protected and preserved throughout the case.
Here we can see the right cartilaginous edges of the trachea along with the ET tube in the area of the injury.
We planned to do a transverse repair, but at this time there was too much tension.
So we decided to perform a mediastinal release and started by taking down the inferior pulmonary ligament
We continued our dissection up the mediastinum, much like how it is commonly done when dissecting out level seven lymph nodes.
Here you can see the improvement in the amount of tension, the edges coming together easily.
We debrided the edges of the cartilage and commenced with our repair.
We used Vicrol sutures, which we threw in a figure of eight fashion.
There were numerous throws in order to ensure an airtight seal.
Once the trachea was repaired, we buttressed it by closing the pleura on top.
Hemostasis was insured and a chest tube was placed.
